channel.title: NYT > World > Americas
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Venezuelans Criticize Own Government Response After Twin Earthquakes
item.description: Venezuelans are publicly expressing their anger over their government’s disaster response, despite the risk of imprisonment.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: People look for their relatives in the rubble of a public housing complex in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday, after twin earthquakes destroyed it.

item.title: How a Four-Generation Cuban Family Survives on $60 a Month
item.description: The U.S. oil blockade has deepened a humanitarian crisis, forcing Cubans to lean on the island’s long tradition of community solidarity to provide a cushion.
item.dc:creator: Ed Augustin and Lisette Poole González
item.media:description: Analeidis Arias Marin gets her children ready for school in Santiago de Cuba in May.

item.title: As Messi Dominates the World Cup, a War Is Waged Over Maradona’s Image
item.description: A multimillion-dollar battle for a soccer god’s brand is being waged across continents, burning investors and tearing his family apart.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and Lucía Cholakian Herrera
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios for The New York Times
item.media:description: Portraits of Argentina’s Lionel Messi and Diego Maradona at a match in Dallas last month.

item.title: Indio Solari, Argentine Rocker Who Packed Stadiums, Dies at 77
item.description: A counterculture icon with broad appeal, he compared himself to Bob Dylan and developed an especially fervent following among working-class youth.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter
item.media:credit: Luis Abdala/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Indio Solari at his last public concert, in 2017. Phrases from his cryptic songs, filled with fictional characters and esoteric metaphors, found their way onto tattoos and T-shirts across Argentina.

item.title: Canada Bets on NATO Partners for Its Expanded Submarine Fleet
item.description: In selecting a joint bid from Germany and Norway, Prime Minister Mark Carney is elevating Canada’s naval power and reducing its military and economic dependency on the United States.
item.dc:creator: Ian Austen
item.media:credit: Chris Helgren/Reuters
item.media:description: Naval officers from Canada touring a South Korean KSS-III submarine in Esquimalt, British Columbia, in May.

item.title: Nancy Ward, the Cherokee Leader, Stokes Division Even 250 Years Later
item.description: In 1776, Cherokee leaders looked for ways to navigate a foreboding future. Nanyehi, a.k.a. Nancy Ward, is venerated and reviled for her choice.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:description: Becky Hobbs, a descendant of Nancy Ward, staunchly defends her ancestor’s legacy.

item.title: Sites That Report Venezuela’s Missing Rise Far From Quake Zone
item.description: Despite problems like duplicate submissions, tallies gathered by the websites have been cited by news organizations and critics of the Venezuelan government’s disaster response.
item.dc:creator: Ishani Desai
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers, relatives and rescue workers search for earthquake survivors under a collapsed public housing complex in Caraballeda, Venezuela, last month, The disaster has bared accuracy problems in how websites gather information on the missing.

item.title: With $8 Billion in Venezuelan Oil Money, U.S. Gives $300 Million in Quake Aid
item.description: In 2010, there was a huge U.S. aid effort after a quake in Haiti. Today, U.S. officials say they oversee billions in Venezuelan oil revenue, but aid is less.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Volunteers and family members searched for survivors in a building in La Guaira, Venezuela, that collapsed after back-to-back earthquakes struck the country in June.

item.title: The World Cup Gave Mexicans a Fantasy They Won’t Soon Forget
item.description: The Mexican team’s early success united and electrified the nation, giving fans a reason to fill the streets in celebration instead of protest.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja and James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mexican fans during the World Cup round of 16 match against England at the Azteca Stadium on Sunday.

item.title: Years of Warnings About Public Housing Preceded Venezuela’s Earthquakes
item.description: Residents, construction experts and seismologists said for years that Venezuela’s public housing would be vulnerable in a natural disaster.
item.dc:creator: Julie Turkewitz, María Victoria Fermín and Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: Canada Has a New Obsession: Soccer
item.description: A surprising World Cup run ended on Saturday against Morocco, but the Canadian successes, as a team and a host, are likely to endure.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.media:credit: Ian Willms for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans cheered on players as Canada played Bosnia and Herzegovina in Toronto last month in the first game of the 2026 World Cup set in Canada.

item.title: A Big Week in the West, Canada vs. Morocco, Trade and a Canada-U.S. Quiz
item.description: Prime Minister Mark Carney further diversified trade away from the U.S. this week by announcing multibillion dollar investments in Alberta and British Columbia.
item.dc:creator: Shawna Richer
item.media:credit: Evan Vucci/Associated Press, via Ap Photo/Evan Vucci
item.media:description: Prime Minister Mark Carney and President Trump in the Oval Office on Oct. 7, 2025.

item.title: Mexico’s Secret 12th Man in the World Cup: Baby Jesus
item.description: The tradition of dressing up a baby Jesus statue began in a Mexico City church nearly 60 years ago. It moved to the cathedral for this World Cup and Mexico hasn’t lost yet.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Baby Jesus’s soccer uniform is size 0.

item.title: Trump’s Travel Crackdown Has a Winner: Mexican Tourism
item.description: While critics question just how much the World Cup will benefit tourism long term, Mexican officials believe it can help boost the country to fifth most visited in the world.
item.dc:creator: James Wagner
item.media:credit: Cesar Rodriguez for The New York Times
item.media:description: Fans at a festival in Mexico City when Mexico was playing South Africa on June 11. The government has tried to position Mexico as a destination for Latin America during the World Cup.

item.title: More Than 1 Million Without Power After Storms in Midwest and Northeast
item.description: Strong winds from thunderstorms began to cause widespread power losses on Friday. More than 1.3 million customers, from Oklahoma to Massachusetts, had no electricity late Saturday.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon
item.media:credit: Robert Killips/Lansing State Journal, via USA Today, via Imagn Images, via Reuters
item.media:description: Large branches came down during a storm in Lansing, Mich., on Friday.

item.title: Defeated at the World Cup, Teams Sometimes Face Abuse and Violence
item.description: Anger and disappointment are not unusual for fans whose teams are knocked out. But some responses have been extreme.
item.dc:creator: Qasim Nauman
item.media:credit: Ahn Young-Joon/Associated Press

item.title: Toronto hosts its last game. Portugal versus Croatia is a great way to go out.
item.dc:creator: Vjosa Isai
item.media:credit: Jeenah Moon/Reuters
item.media:description: Toronto hosted its last World Cup match on Thursday night, when Portugal played Croatia in the round of 32.

item.title: Deadly MV Hondius Hantavirus Outbreak Is Over, W.H.O. Says
item.description: The rodent-borne virus, which sickened passengers on the MV Hondius, killed three people, led to a race to find its origin and a global health alert.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: Hannah Mckay/Reuters
item.media:description: The MV Hondius had only a couple of dozen passengers and crew members as it left the port in Tenerife, one of the Canary Islands, on May 11. It began its journey six weeks earlier in Argentina with about 150 passengers.

item.title: Carney Unveils Multibillion Dollar Oil Pipeline Project to Boost Trade With Asia
item.description: Multibillion dollar investments, including a new oil pipeline from Alberta to the Pacific Coast, and natural gas facilities, aim to diversify trade away from the United States.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:credit: Todd Korol/The Canadian Press via Associated Press
item.media:description: Canada’s Prime Minister Mark Carney and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith arrive to announce a proposed pipeline from Alberta to the coast of British Columbia on Thursday.

item.title: Man Rescued 8 Days After Quake, a Ray of Joy in Stricken Venezuela
item.description: The 44-year-old security guard was pulled alive from a pancaked basement, offering a fleeting moment of hope amid a soaring death toll.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Sulbarán, Shawn Hubler and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Fernando Vergara/Associated Press
item.media:description: Rescue workers attend to Hernán Alberto Gil Flores after he was pulled from the rubble eight days after he was trapped by twin earthquakes that struck Catia La Mar, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: An Aerial View of Disaster at a Venezuela Housing Project
item.description: What one aerial image of a collapsed building tells us about the aftermath of an earthquake in La Guaira, Venezuela.
item.dc:creator: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez
item.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times

item.title: ¿Y Si Sí? The World Cup Delivers Hope to Mexico
item.description: Mexico’s World Cup run has electrified the nation, drawing hundreds of thousands into the streets in celebrations of collective joy, resilience and hope.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas and Luis Antonio Rojas
item.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times

item.title: As Earthquake Death Toll Mounts, Venezuela Grapples With Recovering and Burying Bodies
item.description: As the death toll mounts, thousands of bodies have overwhelmed Venezuela’s forensic system, turning a seaport into a temporary morgue.
item.dc:creator: Tibisay Romero, Isayen Herrera, Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Genevieve Glatsky
item.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
item.media:description: The authorities have started an improvised morgue, so that relatives can look for the bodies of loved ones among the earthquake’s victims at La Guaira’s port in Venezuela.

item.title: Man Killed by Crocodile at a Popular Resort City in Mexico
item.description: The government described the rare fatal attack in Puerto Vallarta as “deeply unfortunate, unusual and isolated.”
item.dc:creator: Gabe Castro-Root
item.media:credit: Daniel Becerril/Reuters
item.media:description: A beach in Puerto Vallarta in February. The city is a popular tourist destination on the Pacific coast of Mexico.

item.title: Canada Will Compete in Eurovision Song Contest
item.description: The country’s national broadcaster confirmed that it is joining the world’s most popular singing contest next year. Some fans are urging it to send a star like Shania Twain.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Martin Meissner/Associated Press
item.media:description: The Eurovision Song Contest final in Vienna this May.

item.title: 4 People Die During World Cup Celebrations in Mexico City
item.description: A man and two women died from asphyxiation, the authorities said, as crowds celebrated the Mexican team’s victory over Ecuador. Another man died after having epileptic seizures.
item.dc:creator: Ali Watkins
item.media:credit: Armando Vega/Reuters
item.media:description: Aficionados reunidos cerca del monumento al Ángel de la Independencia durante el partido del Mundial entre México y Ecuador, el martes en Ciudad de México.

item.title: After Venezuela Earthquakes, Deportees from the U.S. Are Missing or Found Dead
item.description: Many of the 146 Venezuelans deported from the United States the day the earthquake struck are feared dead.
item.dc:creator: Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Patricia Sulbarán
item.media:credit: Vantor, via Reuters
item.media:description: The damaged state-run holding facility for deportees, on a hilltop in La Guaira, Venezuela, was meant to be a way station for a group that arrived there on Wednesday, June 26. Most of them never made it home.

item.title: A Town in Quebec Is the First Governing Body to Adopt the Universal Declaration of the Rights of Trees
item.description: Terrasse-Vaudreuil has adopted a resolution to protect trees, which its mayor called “indispensable allies,” as living beings.
item.dc:creator: Ephrat Livni
item.media:credit: Hubert Hayaud for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are So Many Cubans Migrating to Guyana?
item.description: Guyana, a tiny, oil-rich, English-speaking South American country, has become a destination for Cubans fleeing desperate conditions. We traveled to the capital, Georgetown, to meet with migrant families and learn more about the lives they left behind.
item.dc:creator: Mimi Dwyer, Alex Pena and Yasu Tsuji
